Curriculum → High-level design interview prep → Application architecture and APIs

HLD learning path

Application architecture and APIs

Choose service boundaries, communication styles, and coordination patterns that fit both the workload and the team operating the system.

Module progress 0%

0 of 5 lessons complete

Next up Lesson 1: Application layer responsibilities

The application layer turns requests into business actions, coordinates data access, and owns the product semantics that infrastructure alone cannot express.

Why this module matters Build one clean mental model before scaling it.

Move through the lessons in order so each trade-off has context before the harder distributed systems decisions show up.

Study target 5 lessons left

Aim to finish one lesson, then explain its core trade-off out loud before moving on.

High-level design interview prep 5 lessons 0/5 complete Follow in order for best results Back to HLD roadmap Start module View lesson runway

Learning rhythm

Study this like a sequenced course, not a reference dump

  1. Preview the runway. Skim every lesson title first so you can see the progression before you dive in.
  2. Do one lesson deeply. Read the summary, inspect the diagram, and identify the sharpest trade-off.
  3. Say it back aloud. Explain the idea in interviewer language before you mark it complete.
  4. Only then advance. Move to the next lesson when you can defend the previous one without the notes open.
HLD sequence Designed for interview recall

Outcomes

What you should walk away with

  • Select application decomposition patterns intentionally
  • Understand service discovery, coordination, and API trade-offs
  • Handle synchronous and real-time communication without hiding costs

Practice flow

How to study each lesson

  • Read the summary and explain the core trade-off in your own words.
  • Study the diagram and identify the critical path or failure boundary.
  • Answer the interview prompts aloud before marking the lesson complete.

Mastery check

How you know it is sticking

  • Clear assumptions and estimated scale.
  • Justified component choices instead of pattern dumping.
  • Awareness of operations, failure modes, and trade-offs.

Lesson runway

Follow the sequence from first principles to fluency

This is the core pattern borrowed from stronger learning products: show the path, make the next step obvious, and keep outcomes visible while the learner moves.

0/5 complete
Lesson 1 Next
Application layer responsibilities

The application layer turns requests into business actions, coordinates data access, and owns the product semantics that infrastructure alone cannot express.

25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections

Monoliths vs. microservices

Choose decomposition based on coordination cost, deployment independence, and team boundaries rather than trend pressure.

25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Lesson 3 Open
Service discovery and coordination

Distributed services need a way to locate healthy instances, coordinate leaders, and react to topology changes without manual reconfiguration.

25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections

API design: REST, RPC, and contracts

Pick communication styles that match workflow complexity, evolution needs, and performance sensitivity.

25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Lesson 5 Open
Real-time delivery: WebSockets, SSE, and long polling

When users expect live updates, choose a delivery model that matches fan-out shape, connection count, and the direction of data flow.

25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections

Lesson 1

Application layer responsibilities

Open

The application layer turns requests into business actions, coordinates data access, and owns the product semantics that infrastructure alone cannot express.

Why it matters

A clean application layer lets you scale capabilities independently without losing clarity around ownership and correctness.

What you will cover

Separate concerns intentionallyStateless compute scales bestCoordinate around domain boundaries

Good interview signal

  • Explain what the application layer owns beyond request forwarding.
  • Prefer stateless services for elasticity.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 2

Monoliths vs. microservices

Open

Choose decomposition based on coordination cost, deployment independence, and team boundaries rather than trend pressure.

Why it matters

Many interview answers become stronger when you can justify why a simple modular monolith is enough for an early phase and what pressures justify later extraction.

What you will cover

What a monolith does wellWhat microservices buy youMigration pressure signals

Good interview signal

  • Show that a modular monolith is often a valid starting point.
  • Extract services for a specific reason, not fashion.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 3

Service discovery and coordination

Open

Distributed services need a way to locate healthy instances, coordinate leaders, and react to topology changes without manual reconfiguration.

Why it matters

Discovery and coordination are invisible when done well, but they shape failover behavior, deployment safety, and the blast radius of outages.

What you will cover

Discovery patternsCoordination patternsFailure thinking

Good interview signal

  • Explain how services find healthy peers.
  • Mention registries, health checks, and local caching.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 4

API design: REST, RPC, and contracts

Open

Pick communication styles that match workflow complexity, evolution needs, and performance sensitivity.

Why it matters

The best API choice is the one that makes contracts understandable, evolvable, and efficient for the given clients.

What you will cover

REST strengthsRPC strengthsDesign contracts, not only endpoints

Good interview signal

  • Choose the API style based on clients and workload.
  • Discuss idempotency, pagination, and versioning.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 5

Real-time delivery: WebSockets, SSE, and long polling

Open

When users expect live updates, choose a delivery model that matches fan-out shape, connection count, and the direction of data flow.

Why it matters

Real-time features are common in interviews because they force you to reason about connection state, pub/sub fan-out, and degraded modes.

What you will cover

Pick by communication patternHandle fan-out and connection stateDegraded modes matter

Good interview signal

  • Choose a protocol based on data direction and scale.
  • Discuss connection management and pub/sub fan-out.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson
